# Accounting Decision Log

## Decision 1: Switch from Cash to Accrual Accounting

- **Date**: December 15, 2025 (effective January 1, 2026)
- **Decided by**: Maria Gutierrez, with recommendation from Rachel Green (CPA)
- **Choice**: Convert from cash-basis to accrual-basis accounting
- **Alternatives considered**: Remaining on cash basis, modified cash basis (accrual for AR/AP only)
- **Reasons**: Riverside Cafe applied for an SBA 7(a) loan in October 2025 to refinance existing debt. The SBA lender (OnPoint Credit Union, loan officer: David Hernandez) required accrual-basis financial statements as a condition of the loan. Additionally, accrual accounting provides a more accurate picture of the cafe's financial position — cash basis was causing revenue mismatches when large catering deposits were received weeks before events.
- **Implementation**: Maria Santos performed the conversion in QuickBooks Online by recording a Section 481(a) adjustment. Rachel Green reviewed and approved the adjustment. The conversion created a one-time $8,200 increase in reported income due to outstanding AR being recognized.
- **Cost impact**: No additional software cost. Rachel Green charged a one-time $1,200 fee for the conversion analysis and 481(a) adjustment preparation.

## Decision 2: Hire Part-Time Bookkeeper

- **Date**: November 2025
- **Decided by**: Maria and Jorge Gutierrez
- **Choice**: Hire Maria Santos as a part-time bookkeeper (20 hours/week, $28/hour)
- **Alternatives considered**: Continue self-managing books (Jorge was spending 10+ hours/week), outsource to a bookkeeping service (Bench.co quoted $450/month), hire a full-time office manager ($45K/year)
- **Reasons**: Jorge was spending too much time on bookkeeping instead of managing kitchen operations, leading to both financial errors and food quality issues. Maria Santos was referred by Rachel Green — she has 8 years of bookkeeping experience, is QuickBooks ProAdvisor certified, and lives in the NW Portland neighborhood. At 20 hours/week ($2,240/month), she is more expensive than Bench.co but provides on-site presence for receipt management, vendor communication, and inventory reconciliation.
- **Start date**: January 6, 2026
- **Results**: Since hiring Maria Santos, month-end close time has decreased from 15 days to 8 days, and Jorge has reclaimed approximately 8 hours/week for kitchen management.

## Decision 3: QuickBooks Online over Xero

- **Date**: Originally selected in 2020, reaffirmed in January 2026 during accrual conversion
- **Decided by**: Rachel Green (CPA) recommendation, approved by Maria Gutierrez
- **Choice**: QuickBooks Online Plus ($80/month)
- **Alternatives considered**: Xero Growing plan ($42/month), Wave (free), FreshBooks Plus ($30/month)
- **Reasons**: QuickBooks Online has direct bank feed integration with OnPoint Credit Union (Xero does not — would require manual CSV uploads). Rachel Green's firm uses QuickBooks Online Accountant, enabling her to access the Riverside Cafe books directly for review without needing separate login credentials. QBO's inventory tracking module is adequate for the cafe's needs. The Plus plan supports up to 5 users (Maria Gutierrez, Jorge Gutierrez, Maria Santos, Rachel Green, and one spare).
- **Trade-off**: QBO is nearly twice the price of Xero, but the bank integration and accountant access features save an estimated 3-4 hours/month in manual work.

## Decision 4: S-Corp Election

- **Date**: October 2023 (Form 2553 filed, effective January 1, 2024)
- **Decided by**: Rachel Green (CPA) recommendation
- **Choice**: Elect S-Corporation tax treatment for the LLC
- **Reasons**: With net income consistently above $50K, the S-Corp election saves approximately $5,500/year in self-employment tax. Under sole proprietorship taxation, all net income is subject to 15.3% self-employment tax. Under S-Corp, only the "reasonable salary" ($48,000) is subject to payroll taxes; the remaining profit is distributed as dividends not subject to SE tax.
- **Requirements**: Must pay Maria a "reasonable" W-2 salary. Rachel Green determined $48,000 is defensible based on Bureau of Labor Statistics data for restaurant managers in Portland, OR (median: $52,000). She recommends increasing to $52,000 in 2026 to stay within the safe harbor range.
